'''Brad Mackay'''  (born 7 February 1969 in [[Sydney]], New South Wales) is an Australian former [[rugby league]] footballer who played in the 1980s and 1990s. A versatile {{rlp|lk}} for the [[St George Dragons]], [[Illawarra Steelers]], [[WA Reds|Western Reds]] and the joint-venture of [[St. George Illawarra Dragons]], he also represented [[New South Wales Rugby League team|New South Wales]] in the [[Rugby League State of Origin|State of Origin]] and [[Australian national rugby league team|Australia]].

==Playing career==
A St George junior from the Brighton Seagulls club, Mackay was graded by the Dragons in 1987 and then played 117 games with club over eight seasons until 1994 scoring 22 tries. Playing all positions in the backline bar halfback, he was most remembered for his lock position in his peak.

Mackay debuted for the [[New South Wales Rugby League team|New South Wales Blues]] as a reserve in game II of the [[1989 State of Origin series]]. He went on to play 17 [[Rugby League State of Origin|Origin]] games between 1989 and 1995, scoring three tries at that level.

Mackay had his best season in [[1990 NSWRL season|1990]], making his test début for [[Australian Kangaroos|Australia]] in the mid-season test against [[France national rugby league team|France]], becoming only the second player to score a hat trick of tries on début for Australia. A month after the France test, Mackay went on to play in a one-off test against [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]]. Following the season he was selected for the [[1990 Kangaroo Tour]], coming into the test team for the winning 2nd and 3rd [[The Ashes (rugby league)|Ashes Tests]] against [[Great Britain Lions|Great Britain]] before playing in the two tests against France.

After failing to hold his test place in 1991, Mackay was selected for the first two tests against Great Britain during the Lions [[1992 Great Britain Lions tour of Australasia|1992 tour of Australasia]] but missed the last match of the series through injury. After playing in a [[1989-1992 Rugby League World Cup|World Cup qualifying]] match against [[Papua New Guinea Kumuls|Papua New Guinea]] in [[Townsville]], Mackay was used as a replacement in the Dragons 8-28 [[1992 NSWRL season#Grand final|Grand Final]] loss to the [[Brisbane Broncos]] and missed a place in Australia's World Cup Final winning team.

1993 saw Mackay continue his good form and he was the starting lock forward for NSW in all three [[1993 State of Origin series|State of Origin]] games in the Blues 2–1 series win over [[Queensland Maroons|Queensland]]. The return from injury of [[Bradley Clyde]] would see Mackay on the bench for Australia in the 1st and 3rd Trans-Tasman Tests against New Zealand.

Later that year he became one of only four players (the others being Bradley Clyde in [[1991 NSWRL season#Grand final|1991]], [[Daly Cherry-Evans]] in [[2013 NRL Grand Final|2013]] and [[Jack Wighton]] in [[2019 NRL Grand Final|2019]]) to win the [[Clive Churchill Medal]] from the losing side when the Dragons went down 6–14 in the [[1993 NSWRL season#Grand Final|Grand Final]], again to the [[Brisbane Broncos]].

St. George's form dropped markedly in 1994 and Mackay could only make the Bench for NSW in their [[1994 State of Origin series]] win over [[Queensland Maroons|Queensland]], with NSW coach [[Phil Gould (rugby league)|Phil Gould]] preferring Bradley Clyde at lock. He made his final test appearance for Australia from the interchange bench in the [[1994 French rugby league Oceania tour|1994 mid-season test]] against France at [[Parramatta Stadium]], but the Dragons poor form, which saw them fall to 11th on the [[1994 NSWRL season#Ladder|premiership ladder]], saw Mackay overlooked for the [[1994 Kangaroo Tour]] bringing down the curtain on his international career.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/ |title=Home |publisher=Rugby League Project}}</ref>

Mackay decided to leave the Dragons due to a poor relationship with coach [[Brian Smith (rugby league, born 1954)|Brian Smith]], later saying, "Brian had signed an extension on his contract and so I felt like I needed a change or I'd go down the drain as a player."<ref>{{cite journal|author = Adam Burnett|title = Legend Q&A|journal = [[Rugby League Week]]|issue =13 October 2010|
pages = 38–39|publisher = PBLMedia|location = Sydney, NSW}}</ref>

Mackay joined the [[Western Reds]] in [[Australian Rugby League season 1995|1995]], accepting the foundation captaincy of the [[Perth]]-based side, but moved back east when they joined the [[Super League (Australia)|Super League]] franchise from 1996.

He started playing for the [[Illawarra Steelers]] in [[Australian Rugby League season 1996|1996]], staying for three seasons over which he played 56 games. A return to his best form and season-long consistency in 1997 earned him the BHP Medal as the Steelers' player of the year. Mackay later became part of the new St. George when the joint venture was formed in 1999, playing 24 games, including an appearance from the bench in the [[1999 NRL Grand Final]] loss to the [[Melbourne Storm]].<ref>Alan Whiticker. Encyclopedia of Rugby League Players. 2005 edition</ref>

Mackay had played a total of 248 first grade games over 12 years.

Mackay played out his career in the 2000 season with the [[Bradford Bulls]] in the UK, winning the [[Challenge Cup]] Final with them.

==Post playing==
Brad Mackay is a committed Christian. In 2003, he was active in fund raising for the Joanne Mackay Helping Hand Foundation following the 2002 passing of wife, Joanne from breast cancer.
He lives in the Illawarra area with his second wife, Tracy and is a fireman.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://m.nrl.com/where-are-they-now-brad-the-fireman-mackay/tabid/10874/newsid/62837/default.aspx |title=Where Are They Now: Brad 'The Fireman' Mackay – NRL.com |access-date=13 May 2016 |archive-date=10 June 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160610154437/http://m.nrl.com/where-are-they-now-brad-the-fireman-mackay/tabid/10874/newsid/62837/default.aspx |url-status=dead }}</ref>
